title: Titanic — Tidymodels Tour
text:
This site is a guided tour of the Kaggle Titanic — Machine Learning from Disaster competition, worked end to end in R with the tidymodels framework. It is written for an R user who is comfortable with the language and with statistics, but is new to Kaggle — so we narrate the competition workflow as well as the modelling reasoning, and we lean on ggplot2 for every chart.
If you have taught or sat through an intro-to-ML workshop, this will feel familiar: split, engineer features, resample, tune, evaluate, predict. Kaggle just adds a public leaderboard and a strict submission format on top.


Titanic is the canonical “first Kaggle competition” — the Hello, World of predictive modelling. The setup:

You are given a training set (data/raw/train.csv, 891 passengers) where the outcome is known: did each passenger survive (Survived = 1) or not (Survived = 0)?
You are given a test set (data/raw/test.csv, 418 passengers) with the same features but no Survived column.
Your job: predict Survived for every passenger in the test set, write those predictions to a CSV in the required format, and upload it. Kaggle scores your CSV on accuracy against the hidden true labels and places you on the leaderboard.

There is no prize money here — it’s a sandbox. That is exactly why it’s a great place to have fun and explore: try a model, submit, see your score, iterate.






TipThe Kaggle mental model for a newcomer



Think of it as a take-home exam where you can resubmit as often as you like and see your grade each time (with a daily submission cap). Your cross-validation score on the training data is your private estimate; the leaderboard score is the graded result. When the two diverge, you are learning something about generalization — which is the whole point.

title: Titanic — Tidymodels Tour
text:
The goal
Predict the binary outcome Survived for the 418 test passengers as accurately as possible, and document the reasoning so the whole thing is reproducible and teachable.
We fit three different model families on identical features so they can be compared apples-to-apples:



Model
Engine
Why it’s interesting




XGBoost
xgboost
Gradient-boosted trees — the Kaggle workhorse


Random Forest
ranger
Bagged trees — robust, low-tuning baseline


Neural Net
brulee / nnet
A small MLP — needs normalized inputs

title: Titanic — Tidymodels Tour
text:
How the documents link together
This is a small Quarto website. index.qmd (this page) is the master document; everything else lives in docs/ and the navbar ties them into a narrative:
Home (index)  →  EDA  →  Models {XGBoost, Random Forest, Neural Net}  →  Submission  →  Kaggle Setup

Exploratory Data Analysis — look at the data, find the patterns (sex, class, age, family, title), and motivate the feature engineering.
Model docs — each one follows the same structure (recipe → spec → workflow → tune → finalize → evaluate → save), so once you’ve read one, the others are easy to skim for the differences.
Submission — a single parameterized document that turns any of the three saved models into a leaderboard-ready CSV.
Kaggle Setup — how to authenticate the Kaggle CLI so you can download the data and push submissions.

title: Titanic — Tidymodels Tour
text:
Project structure
titantic/
├── _quarto.yml              # website config (theme, navbar, freeze)
├── index.qmd                # ← you are here (master document)
├── renv.lock                # pinned package versions (reproducibility)
├── R/
│   ├── 00_setup.R           # libraries, seed=42, paths, parallel backend
│   ├── 01_eda.R             # standalone EDA script
│   └── 02_features.R        # make_recipe(data) — the SHARED feature recipe
├── data/
│   └── raw/
│       ├── train.csv        # 891 rows, has Survived
│       └── test.csv         # 418 rows, no Survived
├── docs/
│   ├── 01-eda.qmd           # exploratory analysis
│   ├── 02-xgboost.qmd       # boosted trees
│   ├── 03-random-forest.qmd # ranger random forest
│   ├── 04-neural-net.qmd    # brulee/nnet MLP
│   ├── submission.qmd       # parameterized: build a submission CSV
│   └── setup-kaggle.md      # Kaggle CLI auth guide
├── models/                  # fitted workflows (*_fit.rds) + submission_*.csv
└── figures/                 # exported plots
The single most important shared piece is R/02_features.R, which defines make_recipe(data). Every model sources it so they all train on the same engineered features — that is what makes the comparison fair.

title: Titanic — Tidymodels Tour
text:
How to reproduce
Everything is pinned with renv and uses here::here() for paths, so there is no setwd() fragility.

Restore the environment (installs the exact package versions):
renv::restore()
Get the data — follow the Kaggle setup guide to authenticate, then download train.csv and test.csv into data/raw/.
Render the site. Because of freeze: auto, the heavy model fits only run the first time (or when a model doc changes):
quarto render
Logical run order if you prefer to step through it: EDA → each model doc (which saves its fitted workflow to models/) → submission.
Generate submissions for each model (see below).







Note



set.seed(42) is set in R/00_setup.R and the resampling folds are seeded, so CV results are reproducible across machines that share the renv lockfile.

title: Titanic — Tidymodels Tour
text:
Results summary
The table below compares the four base models on cross-validated accuracy and roc_auc (positive class = "1", survived). These are honest estimates computed on the training data before we ever touch the test set — and they are read directly from each model’s saved tuning results (models/*_res.rds), so they update automatically on every render. No copying.


Show code
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(here)

# Pull the best (roc_auc-selected) configuration's CV metrics from a saved
# tune_grid result.
cv_metrics <- function(path, label, notes) {
  res  <- readRDS(path)
  best <- select_best(res, metric = "roc_auc")
  m    <- collect_metrics(res) |> semi_join(best, by = names(best))
  tibble(
    Model         = label,
    `CV accuracy` = m$mean[m$.metric == "accuracy"],
    `CV ROC-AUC`  = m$mean[m$.metric == "roc_auc"],
    `Best num_comp` = best$num_comp,
    Notes         = notes
  )
}

bind_rows(
  cv_metrics(here("models", "xgboost_res.rds"), "XGBoost",
             "tuned trees / tree_depth / learn_rate / min_n + num_comp"),
  cv_metrics(here("models", "rf_res.rds"), "Random Forest",
             "tuned mtry / min_n + num_comp; 1000 trees"),
  cv_metrics(here("models", "nnet_res.rds"), "Neural Net",
             "tuned hidden_units / penalty + num_comp; PCA-normalized"),
  cv_metrics(here("models", "logistic_res.rds"), "Logistic Regression",
             "elastic-net (glmnet): tuned penalty / mixture + num_comp")
) |>
  arrange(desc(`CV ROC-AUC`)) |>
  mutate(across(c(`CV accuracy`, `CV ROC-AUC`), \(x) round(x, 4))) |>
  knitr::kable(align = "lrrrl")

title: Titanic — Tidymodels Tour
section: The submission workflow at a glance
text:
The submission workflow at a glance
Once a model is fitted and saved (each model doc does this), turning it into a leaderboard entry is mechanical:

Load the fitted workflow from models/<model>_fit.rds.
Read data/raw/test.csv.
predict() the class for each test passenger.
Write models/submission_<model>.csv with exactly two columns, PassengerId and Survived (0/1 integers).
Push it to Kaggle:
kaggle competitions submit -c titanic \
  -f models/submission_xgboost.csv \
  -m "xgboost via tidymodels"

All of that is wrapped in the parameterized submission document, so you generate any model’s CSV with one command:
quarto render docs/submission.qmd -P model:xgboost
quarto render docs/submission.qmd -P model:rf
quarto render docs/submission.qmd -P model:nnet

title: Titanic — Tidymodels Tour
text:

title: Building a Kaggle Submission
text: Where we are: The final stop on the Titanic — Tidymodels Tour. Each model document (XGBoost, Random Forest, Neural Net) fits a workflow and saves it to models/<model>_fit.rds. This document is parameterized: tell it which model, and it loads that fit, predicts on the test set, and writes a Kaggle-ready CSV.

title: Building a Kaggle Submission
section: How to render this document per model
text:
How to render this document per model
This .qmd has a model parameter (default "xgboost"). Override it on the command line with -P model:<value>:
# XGBoost (also the default if you omit -P)
quarto render docs/submission.qmd -P model:xgboost

# Random forest
quarto render docs/submission.qmd -P model:rf

# Neural net
quarto render docs/submission.qmd -P model:nnet
Each render reads the matching models/<model>_fit.rds, writes models/submission_<model>.csv, and prints the exact upload command. You must have fitted (rendered) the corresponding model document first so the .rds exists.

title: Building a Kaggle Submission
text:
Setup


Show code
# We need the tidymodels stack to load the saved workflow and call predict().
# 00_setup.R also sets paths and the seed.
source(here::here("R", "00_setup.R"))
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(here)

title: Building a Kaggle Submission
section: Resolve and validate the requested model
text:
Resolve and validate the requested model


Show code
# The parameter comes in from the YAML default or the -P flag on the CLI.
requested_model <- params$model

# Map each valid model key to its saved fit. This single lookup table is the
# source of truth for which models are submittable.
model_paths <- c(
  xgboost  = here::here("models", "xgboost_fit.rds"),
  rf       = here::here("models", "rf_fit.rds"),
  nnet     = here::here("models", "nnet_fit.rds"),
  logistic = here::here("models", "logistic_fit.rds"),
  stack    = here::here("models", "stack_fit.rds")
)

# Fail loudly and helpfully if someone passes an unknown model.
if (!requested_model %in% names(model_paths)) {
  stop(
    "Unknown model '", requested_model, "'. ",
    "Choose one of: ", paste(names(model_paths), collapse = ", "), ". ",
    "Example: quarto render docs/submission.qmd -P model:rf",
    call. = FALSE
  )
}

fit_path <- model_paths[[requested_model]]

# Make sure the fitted workflow actually exists — i.e. the model doc was
# rendered first.
if (!file.exists(fit_path)) {
  stop(
    "Fitted model not found at '", fit_path, "'. ",
    "Render the matching model document first (e.g. quarto render ",
    "docs/02-xgboost.qmd) so the .rds is saved.",
    call. = FALSE
  )
}

message("Building submission for model: ", requested_model)

title: Building a Kaggle Submission
text:
Predict and assemble the submission


Show code
# predict() runs the workflow's recipe on the raw test data, then the model.
# type = "class" returns the hard 0/1 label Kaggle wants.
preds <- predict(model_fit, new_data = test, type = "class")

# Kaggle requires EXACTLY two columns: PassengerId and Survived, with Survived
# as 0/1 integers. .pred_class is a factor ("0"/"1"); convert via character so
# we don't accidentally get factor codes (1/2).
submission <- tibble(
  PassengerId = test$PassengerId,
  Survived    = as.integer(as.character(preds$.pred_class))
)

# Sanity checks: right number of rows, right value set, no missing predictions.
stopifnot(
  nrow(submission) == nrow(test),
  all(submission$Survived %in% c(0L, 1L)),
  !anyNA(submission$Survived)
)

head(submission, 10)

text:
Write the CSV


Show code
out_path <- here("models", paste0("submission_", requested_model, ".csv"))

write_csv(submission, out_path)

title: Building a Kaggle Submission
text:
Submit to Kaggle
Push the CSV to the leaderboard with the Kaggle CLI. The command below is built for the model you rendered:
kaggle competitions submit -c titanic -f models/submission_nnet.csv -m "nnet via tidymodels"






NoteFirst time? Authenticate first



The kaggle CLI needs your API token (~/.kaggle/kaggle.json) before any submit or download will work. The Kaggle setup guide walks through creating and installing it.








TipDaily submission limit



Kaggle caps Titanic submissions per day (commonly ~10). Submit your best candidate by CV score rather than spraying every experiment at the leaderboard.

title: Model 5 — Logistic Regression
text: Where we are: A fourth base learner for the Titanic — Tidymodels Tour, following the same recipe → spec → workflow → tune → finalize → evaluate → save structure as the XGBoost, Random Forest and Neural Net docs. It also feeds the stacked ensemble.

title: Model 5 — Logistic Regression
text:
Why logistic regression?
It is the classic starting point for binary classification: a linear model of the log-odds of survival. With the glmnet engine we get elastic-net regularisation — penalty (how much to shrink) and mixture (ridge ↔︎ lasso) — which guards against overfitting the wide, PCA-augmented feature space and does automatic feature selection. It is fast, interpretable (signed coefficients), and a strong, honest baseline the fancier models must beat.

title: Model 5 — Logistic Regression
text:
Setup


Show code
source(here::here("R", "00_setup.R"))      # tidyverse + tidymodels, seed = 42
source(here("R", "02_features.R"))         # make_recipe(data)

library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(here)
library(glmnet)   # penalised logistic regression engine




Show code
train <- read_csv(here("data", "processed", "train.csv")) |>
  mutate(Survived = factor(Survived, levels = c("0", "1")))

title: Model 5 — Logistic Regression
text:
The recipe
The same shared recipe as every other model — a fair comparison on identical features (including the principal components).


Show code
logistic_recipe <- make_recipe(train)
logistic_recipe





── Recipe ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────





── Inputs 


Number of variables by role


outcome:    1
predictor: 22
id:         1





── Operations 


• Variables removed: Name, Ticket, Cabin, Surname


• K-nearest neighbor imputation for: Age


• Median imputation for: Fare FarePerPerson


• Mode imputation for: Embarked


• Variable mutation for: as.character(Pclass)


• Collapsing factor levels for: Title


• Novel factor level assignment for: all_nominal_predictors()


• Dummy variables from: all_nominal_predictors()

• Centering and scaling for: all_numeric_predictors()

title: Model 5 — Logistic Regression
text:
The model specification


Show code
# Elastic-net logistic regression: penalty = shrinkage strength, mixture = the
# ridge(0) <-> lasso(1) balance. Both tuned.
logistic_spec <- logistic_reg(
  penalty = tune(),
  mixture = tune()
) |>
  set_engine("glmnet") |>
  set_mode("classification")

logistic_spec


Logistic Regression Model Specification (classification)

Main Arguments:
  penalty = tune()
  mixture = tune()

Computational engine: glmnet

title: Model 5 — Logistic Regression
Model 5 — Logistic Regression
text:
Tuning with cross-validation


Show code
set.seed(42)
folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 10, strata = Survived)




Show code
# The recipe adds PCA internally, so num_comp is tunable too. finalize() against
# a prepped predictor set resolves any data-dependent ranges.
preds <- prep(make_recipe(train, num_comp = 2)) |>
  bake(new_data = NULL) |>
  select(-Survived, -PassengerId)

logistic_params <- extract_parameter_set_dials(logistic_wf) |>
  finalize(preds) |>
  update(num_comp = num_comp(range = c(2L, 10L)))

set.seed(42)
logistic_grid <- grid_space_filling(logistic_params, size = 18)




Show code
logistic_metrics <- metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy)

set.seed(42)
logistic_tuned <- tune_grid(
  logistic_wf,
  resamples = folds,
  grid      = logistic_grid,
  metrics   = logistic_metrics,
  control   = control_grid(save_pred = TRUE, save_workflow = TRUE, event_level = "second")
)




Show code
saveRDS(logistic_tuned, here("models", "logistic_res.rds"))



Tuning results


Show code
autoplot(logistic_tuned, metric = "roc_auc")





ROC-AUC across the penalty / mixture grid (autoplot).






Show code
show_best(logistic_tuned, metric = "roc_auc", n = 5)

title: Model 5 — Logistic Regression
section: Finalize and fit on the full training set
text:
Finalize and fit on the full training set


Show code
best_logistic <- select_best(logistic_tuned, metric = "roc_auc")
best_logistic



  



Show code
final_logistic_wf <- finalize_workflow(logistic_wf, best_logistic)

set.seed(42)
logistic_fit <- fit(final_logistic_wf, data = train)

title: Model 5 — Logistic Regression
text:
Cross-validated performance


Show code
collect_metrics(logistic_tuned) |>
  semi_join(best_logistic, by = names(best_logistic)) |>
  select(.metric, mean, std_err, n)



  









Tip



These CV metrics feed the results table on the master document automatically — it reads models/logistic_res.rds at render time, so there’s nothing to copy by hand.



Confusion matrix


Show code
logistic_cv_preds <- collect_predictions(logistic_tuned, parameters = best_logistic)

logistic_cv_preds |>
  conf_mat(truth = Survived, estimate = .pred_class) |>
  autoplot(type = "heatmap") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = kakapo_pal[1]) +
  labs(title = "Logistic Regression — CV confusion matrix")


Scale for fill is already present.
Adding another scale for fill, which will replace the existing scale.





Confusion matrix from out-of-fold CV predictions.






Coefficients (the linear story)
Unlike the tree models, a logistic regression hands us signed effects: the log-odds contribution of each (standardised) feature at the chosen penalty. Positive pushes toward survival, negative toward not.


Show code
logistic_fit |>
  extract_fit_parsnip() |>
  tidy() |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)", estimate != 0) |>
  slice_max(abs(estimate), n = 15) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(term, abs(estimate)), y = estimate,
             fill = estimate > 0)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = kakapo_pal[1], `FALSE` = kakapo_pal[5]),
                    labels = c(`TRUE` = "toward survival", `FALSE` = "toward death"),
                    name = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Logistic Regression coefficients",
       x = NULL, y = "Coefficient (log-odds)") +
  theme_minimal()





Largest elastic-net coefficients (log-odds). Green = toward survival.




The same engineered signals — GroupSurvival, Title, Sex, class — carry the weight, now with an explicit direction.

title: Model 5 — Logistic Regression
text:
Save the fitted workflow


Show code
saveRDS(logistic_fit, file = here("models", "logistic_fit.rds"))

title: Model 5 — Logistic Regression
text:
Submission

title: Model 2 — Random Forest
text: Where we are: Second of three model documents in the Titanic — Tidymodels Tour. It follows the same structure as the XGBoost doc — recipe → spec → workflow → tune → finalize → evaluate → save — so skim for the differences. Features come from the EDA via the shared make_recipe().

title: Model 2 — Random Forest
text:
Why a random forest?
Where XGBoost grows trees sequentially to correct residuals, a random forest grows many independent deep trees on bootstrap samples and averages them. It is famously forgiving: with trees = 1000 fixed, there are really only two knobs worth tuning (mtry, min_n), it rarely overfits badly, and it gives a strong baseline with almost no fuss. We use the fast ranger engine and ask it for permutation importance.

The model specification
trees is fixed at 1000 (more is rarely worth it); we tune mtry and min_n. We set importance = "permutation" so we can plot variable importance later.


Show code

                         min_n = tune(),
                         trees = 1000) |>
    set_engine("ranger", importance = "permutation") |>
    set_mode("classification")


rf_spec

Main Arguments:

  min_n = tune()

Computational engine: ranger

title: Model 2 — Random Forest
Model 2 — Random Forest
text:
Tuning with 5-fold cross-validation


Show code
set.seed(42)
folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 10, strata = Survived)




Show code
# mtry's upper bound depends on the number of predictors AFTER the recipe runs,
# so we finalize() its range against a prepped predictor set rather than
# guessing. num_comp (PCA) is tuned over a sensible range.
preds <- prep(make_recipe(train, num_comp = 2)) |>
  bake(new_data = NULL) |>
  select(-Survived, -PassengerId)

rf_params <- extract_parameter_set_dials(rf_wf) |>
  finalize(preds) |>
  update(num_comp = num_comp(range = c(2L, 10L)))

set.seed(42)
rf_grid <- grid_space_filling(rf_params, size = 18)




Show code
rf_metrics <- metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy)

set.seed(42)
rf_tuned <- tune_grid(
  rf_wf,
  resamples = folds,
  grid      = rf_grid,
  metrics   = rf_metrics,
  # Positive class "1" is the second factor level.
  control   = control_grid(save_pred = TRUE, save_workflow = TRUE, event_level = "second")
)




Show code
saveRDS(rf_tuned, here("models", "rf_res.rds"))



Tuning results


Show code
autoplot(rf_tuned, metric = "roc_auc")





ROC-AUC across the mtry / min_n grid (autoplot).






Show code
show_best(rf_tuned, metric = "roc_auc", n = 5)

title: Model 2 — Random Forest
section: Finalize and fit on the full training set
text:
Finalize and fit on the full training set


Show code
best_rf <- select_best(rf_tuned, metric = "roc_auc")
best_rf



  



Show code
final_rf_wf <- finalize_workflow(rf_wf, best_rf)

set.seed(42)
rf_fit <- fit(final_rf_wf, data = train)

title: Model 2 — Random Forest
text:
Cross-validated performance


Show code
collect_metrics(rf_tuned) |>
  semi_join(best_rf, by = names(best_rf)) |>
  select(.metric, mean, std_err, n)



  









Tip



These CV metrics feed the results table on the master document automatically — it reads models/rf_res.rds at render time, so there’s nothing to copy by hand.



Confusion matrix


Show code
rf_cv_preds <- collect_predictions(rf_tuned, parameters = best_rf)

rf_cv_preds |>
  conf_mat(truth = Survived, estimate = .pred_class) |>
  autoplot(type = "heatmap") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = kakapo_pal[1]) +
  labs(title = "Random Forest — CV confusion matrix")


Scale for fill is already present.
Adding another scale for fill, which will replace the existing scale.





Confusion matrix from out-of-fold CV predictions.






Variable importance


Show code
rf_fit |>
  extract_fit_parsnip() |>
  vip(num_features = 15, geom = "col",
      aesthetics = list(fill = kakapo_pal[1])) +
  labs(title = "Random Forest variable importance (permutation)") +
  theme_minimal()





Permutation importance — drop in accuracy when each feature is shuffled.




Reassuringly, the forest leans on the same engineered features as XGBoost — Title, Sex, Fare, class. Different algorithm, same story from the data.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis
text: Where we are: This is step one of the Titanic — Tidymodels Tour. Before fitting any model we look at the data, find the signal, and let what we see motivate the shared feature recipe in R/02_features.R (make_recipe()). Every model document downstream trains on those features, so the decisions we justify here ripple through the whole project.

text:
Setup


Show code
# Source the project setup: loads tidyverse + tidymodels, sets seed = 42,
# defines path_* helpers, and registers the parallel backend.
source(here::here("R", "00_setup.R"))

# Attach the libraries whose functions are used bare throughout this doc.
library(tidyverse)   # dplyr, ggplot2, readr, stringr, tidyr, purrr, forcats, etc.

# Survived is the outcome. Treat it as a factor with explicit levels.

The headline structural facts:

891 passengers, the outcome Survived is binary.
Age is missing for ~20% of passengers — too much to drop, so we will impute it in the recipe.
Cabin is missing for ~77% — mostly unusable as-is, though its presence/absence may carry signal (a recorded cabin ≈ higher class).
Embarked has 2 missing values — trivially imputable with the mode.



Show code
# A quick ggplot of missingness so we can *see* what skim told us.
train |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ mean(is.na(.x)))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(),
                      names_to = "variable", values_to = "pct_missing") |>
  filter(pct_missing > 0) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(variable, pct_missing),
                               y = pct_missing)) +
  geom_col(fill = kakapo_pal[1]) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(pct_missing, accuracy = 1)),
                     hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent,
                              expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Missingness by column",
                x = NULL, y = "Missing") +
  theme_minimal()





Proportion of missing values per column. Age and Cabin dominate.




The overall survival rate is our base rate — the number to beat with any model:


Show code
train |>
  count(Survived) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n))



  



About 38% survived. A “predict everyone died” baseline would therefore score ~62% accuracy — keep that in mind when judging model scores.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
Who survived? The classic patterns

Survival by sex


Show code
train |>
  group_by(Sex) |>
  summarise(survival_rate = mean(Survived == "1"), n = n()) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Sex, y = survival_rate, fill = Sex)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(survival_rate, accuracy = 1)),
                     vjust = -0.4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = kakapo_pal) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent,
                              limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = "\"Women and children first\"",
                x = NULL, y = "Survival rate") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")





Survival rate by sex. The single strongest predictor in the data.




~74% of women survived vs ~19% of men. Sex is the dominant predictor — any model that ignores it is leaving the table.


Survival by passenger class


Show code
train |>
  mutate(Pclass = factor(Pclass)) |>
  group_by(Pclass) |>
  summarise(survival_rate = mean(Survived == "1")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Pclass, y = survival_rate, fill = Pclass)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(survival_rate, accuracy = 1)),
                     vjust = -0.4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = kakapo_pal) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = "Class mattered",
                x = "Passenger class", y = "Survival rate") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")





Survival rate by passenger class (1 = first class).




A clear gradient: 1st class ~63%, 2nd ~47%, 3rd ~24%. Class is a proxy for deck location, wealth, and access to lifeboats.


Sex and class together


Show code
train |>
  mutate(Pclass = factor(Pclass)) |>
  group_by(Sex, Pclass) |>
  summarise(survival_rate = mean(Survived == "1"), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Pclass, y = survival_rate, fill = Sex)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = kakapo_pal) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = "Sex × class interaction",
                x = "Passenger class", y = "Survival rate") +
  theme_minimal()





Survival by sex within each class — the interaction is striking.




First-class women survived almost universally; third-class men almost never. Tree-based models capture this interaction automatically; for the neural net it helps to have rich, normalized features.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
Age, fare, and continuous structure

Age distribution by survival


Show code
train |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Age, fill = Survived)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, position = "identity", alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0" = kakapo_pal[4], "1" = kakapo_pal[1]),
                             labels = c("Died", "Survived")) +
  labs(title = "Age distribution by outcome",
                x = "Age (years)", y = "Count", fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()





Age distribution split by survival. Note the spike of saved children.






Show code
train |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Age, fill = Survived)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0" = kakapo_pal[4], "1" = kakapo_pal[1]),
                             labels = c("Died", "Survived")) +
  labs(title = "Age density by outcome",
                x = "Age (years)", y = "Density", fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()





Same story as a density — young children survived disproportionately.




Children (roughly under 10) survived at a higher rate — the “and children” half of the rule. Because Age is ~20% missing, imputation in the recipe is essential; a median or bagged-tree imputation keeps these rows in play.


Fare by class


Show code
train |>
  mutate(Pclass = factor(Pclass)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Pclass, y = Fare, fill = Pclass)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.alpha = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = kakapo_pal) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = dollar) +
  labs(title = "Fare by passenger class (log scale)",
                x = "Passenger class", y = "Fare (log10)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")





Fare by class (log scale). Fare is largely a continuous echo of class.




Fare is right-skewed and strongly tied to class — a candidate for a log transform so models (especially the neural net) see a better-behaved distribution.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
Engineered signal: family and title
These two features are not columns in the raw data — we derive them. This is the heart of feature engineering and exactly what make_recipe() will encode.

Family size
SibSp (siblings/spouses aboard) + Parch (parents/children aboard) + 1 (self) gives a family size. Travelling alone vs. in a small vs. large group changes survival odds.


Show code
train |>
  mutate(FamilySize = SibSp + Parch + 1) |>
  group_by(FamilySize) |>
  summarise(survival_rate = mean(Survived == "1"), n = n(),
                   .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = FamilySize, y = survival_rate)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = n), width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = n), vjust = -0.4, size = 3) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_fill_gradientn(colours = kakapo_pal, name = "n passengers") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:11) +
  labs(title = "Survival by family size",
                subtitle = "Solo travellers and large families did worst",
                x = "Family size (SibSp + Parch + 1)", y = "Survival rate") +
  theme_minimal()





Survival rate by family size. Small families fared best.




A clear “Goldilocks” pattern: family size 2–4 survived best, while solo travellers and very large families fared worst. This motivates both a continuous FamilySize feature and possibly a binned IsAlone flag.


Title extracted from name
The Name column embeds a title (Mr, Mrs, Miss, Master, and rarer ones like Dr, Rev, Col). Title bundles sex, age band, and social status into one categorical feature — and Master in particular flags young boys, who survived more often than adult men.


Show code
train |>
  # Pull the title: the word ending in "." after the comma in the Name field.
  mutate(
    Title = str_extract(Name, "(?<=, )[A-Za-z]+(?=\\.)")
  ) |>
  # Collapse the long tail of rare titles so the plot (and later the model)
  # is not dominated by singletons.
  add_count(Title) |>
  mutate(Title = if_else(n < 10, "Rare", Title)) |>
  group_by(Title) |>
  summarise(survival_rate = mean(Survived == "1"), n = n(),
                   .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Title, survival_rate),
                               y = survival_rate)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = n), width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = n), hjust = -0.1, size = 3) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent,
                              limits = c(0, 1.05),
                              expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  scale_fill_gradientn(colours = kakapo_pal, name = "n passengers") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Survival by title",
                x = NULL, y = "Survival rate") +
  theme_minimal()





Survival rate by extracted title (titles with < 10 passengers grouped as ‘Rare’).




Mrs/Miss survived at high rates, Master (boys) noticeably better than Mr. Title is one of the most valuable engineered features in this dataset.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
What this means for make_recipe()
Everything above is the justification for the shared recipe in R/02_features.R. In words, make_recipe(data) should:

Impute Age (missing ~20%) — median or bagged imputation.
Impute Embarked with the mode (only 2 missing).
Derive FamilySize = SibSp + Parch + 1, and optionally an IsAlone flag.
Extract Title from Name and lump rare titles together.
Log-transform Fare (right-skewed) and consider a HasCabin flag from Cabin presence.
Encode factors (Sex, Embarked, Pclass, Title) as dummies for models that need numeric input.
Drop identifiers (PassengerId, Ticket, raw Name, raw Cabin).







Note



The neural-net document adds one more step on top of the shared recipe — step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) — because MLPs are sensitive to input scale in a way that tree ensembles are not. See the Neural Net doc for why.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
Next steps
You now know why the features look the way they do. Time to model:

XGBoost — start here; it’s the Kaggle workhorse and establishes the modelling template the other docs reuse.
Random Forest — a robust, low-tuning comparison.
Neural Net — a small MLP that leans on the normalized recipe.
Back to the master document for the big picture and the results table.

title: Model 1 — XGBoost
text: Where we are: First of three model documents in the Titanic — Tidymodels Tour. All three follow the same structure — recipe → spec → workflow → tune → finalize → evaluate → save — so once you’ve read this one, Random Forest and Neural Net are quick to skim for the differences. The features come from the EDA and the shared make_recipe().

title: Model 1 — XGBoost
text:
Why XGBoost first?
Gradient-boosted decision trees are the default first reach on tabular Kaggle problems. They handle mixed numeric/categorical features, capture interactions (like sex × class) automatically, are robust to monotone transforms, and tune to strong accuracy with modest effort. We tune four of the most impactful knobs.

title: Model 1 — XGBoost
text:
Setup


Show code
# Project setup: tidyverse + tidymodels, seed = 42, paths, parallel backend.
source(here::here("R", "00_setup.R"))
# Shared feature engineering: defines make_recipe(data).
source(here("R", "02_features.R"))

# Attach the libraries used throughout this document.
library(tidyverse)   # dplyr, ggplot2, readr, stringr, tibble, tidyr, purrr
library(tidymodels)  # recipes, parsnip, workflows, tune, yardstick, rsample, dials, broom
library(here)        # path construction
library(vip)         # variable importance plots
library(xgboost)     # the engine




Show code
train <- readr::read_csv(here("data", "processed", "train.csv")) |>
  # Project-wide convention: Survived is a factor, levels c("0","1"),
  # positive class = "1" (so metrics use event_level = "second").
  mutate(Survived = factor(Survived, levels = c("0", "1")))

title: Model 1 — XGBoost
text:
The recipe
The shared recipe encodes everything we justified in the EDA: impute Age/Embarked, derive FamilySize, extract Title, log-transform Fare, dummy-encode factors, drop identifiers.


Show code
xgb_recipe <- make_recipe(train)
xgb_recipe





── Recipe ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────





── Inputs 


Number of variables by role


outcome:    1
predictor: 22
id:         1





── Operations 


• Variables removed: Name, Ticket, Cabin, Surname


• K-nearest neighbor imputation for: Age


• Median imputation for: Fare FarePerPerson


• Mode imputation for: Embarked


• Variable mutation for: as.character(Pclass)


• Collapsing factor levels for: Title


• Novel factor level assignment for: all_nominal_predictors()


• Dummy variables from: all_nominal_predictors()


• Zero variance filter on: all_predictors()


• Centering and scaling for: all_numeric_predictors()


• PCA extraction with: all_numeric_predictors()

title: Model 1 — XGBoost
text:
The model specification
We mark the four tuning parameters with tune(). tidymodels will fill them in during the grid search.


Show code
xgb_spec <- boost_tree(
  trees      = tune(),
  tree_depth = tune(),
  learn_rate = tune(),
  min_n      = tune()
) |>
  set_engine("xgboost") |>
  set_mode("classification")

Model 1 — XGBoost
text:
Tuning with 5-fold cross-validation


Show code
# Stratify on the outcome so each fold preserves the ~38% survival rate.
set.seed(42)
folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 10, strata = Survived)




Show code
# Predictors used to finalize data-dependent params (mtry, num_comp upper bound).
preds <- prep(make_recipe(train, num_comp = 2)) |>
  bake(new_data = NULL) |>
  select(-Survived, -PassengerId)

xgb_params <- extract_parameter_set_dials(xgb_wf) |>
  finalize(preds) |>
  update(num_comp = num_comp(range = c(2L, 10L)))

set.seed(42)
xgb_grid <- grid_space_filling(xgb_params, size = 18)




Show code
# Score every candidate on both metrics. event_level = "second" because the
# positive class ("survived" = "1") is the SECOND factor level.
xgb_metrics <- metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy)

set.seed(42)
xgb_tuned <- tune_grid(
  xgb_wf,
  resamples = folds,
  grid      = xgb_grid,
  metrics   = xgb_metrics,
  control   = control_grid(save_pred = TRUE,
                           save_workflow = TRUE,
                           event_level = "second")
)

title: Model 1 — XGBoost
text:
Cross-validated performance
The fairest estimate of out-of-sample skill is the CV score of the chosen configuration — not the fit on the full training data (which overfits by construction).


Show code
# Collect the CV metrics for the winning hyperparameters.
collect_metrics(xgb_tuned) |>
  semi_join(best_xgb, by = names(best_xgb)) |>
  select(.metric, mean, std_err, n)



  









Tip



These CV metrics feed the results table on the master document automatically — it reads models/xgboost_res.rds at render time, so there’s nothing to copy by hand.



Confusion matrix
Built from the held-out CV predictions for the best configuration — so it reflects generalization, not memorization.


Show code
xgb_cv_preds <- collect_predictions(xgb_tuned, parameters = best_xgb)

xgb_cv_preds |>
  conf_mat(truth = Survived, estimate = .pred_class) |>
  autoplot(type = "heatmap") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = kakapo_pal[1]) +
  labs(title = "XGBoost — CV confusion matrix")


Scale for fill is already present.
Adding another scale for fill, which will replace the existing scale.

  vip(num_features = 15, geom = "col",

  theme_minimal()





Top features by XGBoost gain. Title, Sex, Fare and class dominate.




The engineered Title, plus Sex, Fare, and class, sit at the top — exactly the signal the EDA surfaced. Feature engineering earns its keep.

title: Model 3 — Neural Net
text: Where we are: Last of three model documents in the Titanic — Tidymodels Tour. Same structure as the XGBoost and Random Forest docs — recipe → spec → workflow → tune → finalize → evaluate → save. The one twist: a neural net needs normalized inputs, so this doc extends the shared make_recipe() with a normalization step.

title: Model 3 — Neural Net
text:
Why a neural net here?
On a dataset this small (891 rows) a multilayer perceptron will not beat the tree ensembles — and that’s fine. The point is to explore a different model family and see how tidymodels makes swapping engines almost free. A small MLP with a single hidden layer and weight decay (penalty) is a clean, instructive choice.






NoteEngine choice: nnet



We fit the MLP with base R’s nnet engine. It has no native-code dependency, so it trains identically on your laptop, in the Quarto site build, and in GitHub Actions CI. A torch-backed alternative (brulee) is shown later as an optional local upgrade — but it does not load inside this project’s render/CI session, so nnet is the engine we publish.

title: Model 3 — Neural Net
text:
Setup


Show code
source(here::here("R", "00_setup.R"))      # tidyverse + tidymodels, seed = 42
source(here("R", "02_features.R"))   # make_recipe(data)

library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(here)
library(nnet)     # the MLP engine (base R, no native deps)
library(vip)      # variable importance plots




Show code
train <- read_csv(here("data", "processed", "train.csv")) |>
  mutate(Survived = factor(Survived, levels = c("0", "1")))




Show code
# Tear down the PSOCK cluster from 00_setup.R and tune single-process. The grid
# is tiny, so this is plenty fast and keeps results deterministic (no worker
# package-loading surprises).
if (exists("cl")) try(parallel::stopCluster(cl), silent = TRUE)
registerDoSEQ()

title: Model 3 — Neural Net
text:
The recipe — with normalization
Tree ensembles are invariant to monotone rescaling, so the shared recipe didn’t bother normalizing. A neural net is not invariant: gradient descent behaves far better when every numeric predictor has mean 0 and unit variance, otherwise large-scale features (like Fare) dominate the gradient. So we take the shared recipe and append step_normalize().


Show code
# Extend the shared recipe with normalization of all numeric predictors. This
# step must come AFTER dummy encoding in make_recipe() so the 0/1 dummy columns
# are normalized too (standard practice for MLPs).
nnet_recipe <- make_recipe(train)

nnet_recipe





── Recipe ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────





── Inputs 


Number of variables by role


outcome:    1
predictor: 22
id:         1





── Operations 


• Variables removed: Name, Ticket, Cabin, Surname


• K-nearest neighbor imputation for: Age


• Median imputation for: Fare FarePerPerson


• Mode imputation for: Embarked


• Variable mutation for: as.character(Pclass)


• Collapsing factor levels for: Title


• Novel factor level assignment for: all_nominal_predictors()


• Dummy variables from: all_nominal_predictors()


• Zero variance filter on: all_predictors()


• Centering and scaling for: all_numeric_predictors()


• PCA extraction with: all_numeric_predictors()

title: Model 3 — Neural Net
text:
The model specification


Show code
# Single-hidden-layer MLP via base R's nnet engine: one hidden layer
# (hidden_units) and L2 weight decay (penalty). nnet has no native-code
# dependency, so it trains and renders reliably everywhere — including the
# Quarto site build and GitHub Actions CI.
nnet_spec <- mlp(
  hidden_units = tune(),
  penalty      = tune()
) |>
  set_engine("nnet") |>
  set_mode("classification")

nnet_spec


Single Layer Neural Network Model Specification (classification)

Main Arguments:
  hidden_units = tune()
  penalty = tune()

Computational engine: nnet 








TipOptional: a torch-backed MLP with brulee (local only)



For a deeper net with a tuned epoch budget you can swap in brulee (a torch backend) when running locally. It also exposes epochs:
nnet_spec <- mlp(
  hidden_units = tune(),
  penalty      = tune(),
  epochs       = tune()       # brulee only
) |>
  set_engine("brulee") |>
  set_mode("classification")
We keep nnet as the published engine on purpose: brulee’s libtorch backend does not load inside the Quarto/knitr render session (or in CI) under this project’s renv setup — every fit fails with “Lantern is not loaded”. nnet sidesteps that entirely. If you switch to brulee, add epochs back to the tuning grid below and train sequentially (foreach::registerDoSEQ()).

Scale for fill is already present.
Adding another scale for fill, which will replace the existing scale.





Confusion matrix from out-of-fold CV predictions.






Variable importance
Neural nets don’t expose importance natively. For an nnet model vip derives it from the trained connection weights (Garson’s algorithm) — no shuffling or prediction wrapper needed, so it’s robust in any render environment.


Show code
# Pull the underlying nnet engine object out of the fitted workflow and let vip
# read importance straight from the network weights.
nnet_fit |>
  extract_fit_engine() |>
  vip(num_features = 15, aesthetics = list(fill = kakapo_pal[1])) +
  labs(title = "Neural Net variable importance (Garson weights)") +
  theme_minimal()





Connection-weight (Garson) importance for the MLP.




Even through a different lens, the same features carry the weight — Title, Sex, class, Fare — consistent with XGBoost and the Random Forest.

title: Model 4 — Stacked Ensemble
text: Where we are: The capstone of the Titanic — Tidymodels Tour. The four base-model documents (XGBoost, Random Forest, Neural Net, Logistic Regression) each saved their tuning results (every candidate model and its out-of-fold predictions) to models/<model>_res.rds. Here we feed all of those candidates to stacks, which learns a regularised linear blend over them — usually beating any single model, because the members make different mistakes.

title: Model 4 — Stacked Ensemble
text:
Why stack?
Each model family has a different inductive bias: gradient boosting chases interactions, the random forest averages decorrelated trees, the neural net learns a smooth function of the principal components. A stack fits a meta-learner (penalised logistic regression) on the members’ out-of-fold predictions, keeping only the candidates that pull their weight.

title: Model 4 — Stacked Ensemble
text:
Setup


Show code
source(here::here("R", "00_setup.R"))   # tidyverse + tidymodels, seed = 42

library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(here)
library(stacks)
# The member engines must be attached so fit_members() can refit them.
library(ranger)
library(xgboost)
library(nnet)
library(glmnet)

title: Model 4 — Stacked Ensemble
text:
Load the tuning results


Show code
# Each *_res.rds is a tune_grid result saved with control_grid(save_pred = TRUE,
# save_workflow = TRUE) — exactly what stacks needs to assemble candidates.
xgb_res      <- readRDS(here("models", "xgboost_res.rds"))
rf_res       <- readRDS(here("models", "rf_res.rds"))
nnet_res     <- readRDS(here("models", "nnet_res.rds"))
logistic_res <- readRDS(here("models", "logistic_res.rds"))

title: Model 4 — Stacked Ensemble
section: Build, blend, and fit the ensemble
text:
Build, blend, and fit the ensemble


Show code
set.seed(42)
titanic_stack <- stacks() |>
  add_candidates(xgb_res) |>
  add_candidates(rf_res) |>
  add_candidates(nnet_res) |>
  add_candidates(logistic_res) |>
  blend_predictions() |>     # LASSO over candidates -> keeps the useful ones
  fit_members()              # refit the retained members on all training data

titanic_stack


# A tibble: 6 × 3
  member                               type         weight
  <chr>                                <chr>         <dbl>
1 .pred_1_xgb_respre3_mod14_post0      boost_tree    1.31 
2 .pred_1_rf_respre1_mod05_post0       rand_forest   1.16 
3 .pred_1_nnet_respre7_mod08_post0     mlp           0.939
4 .pred_1_nnet_respre5_mod14_post0     mlp           0.842
5 .pred_1_xgb_respre1_mod10_post0      boost_tree    0.232
6 .pred_1_logistic_respre5_mod14_post0 logistic_reg  0.181



Which members survived the blend?


Show code
autoplot(titanic_stack, type = "weights") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = grDevices::colorRampPalette(kakapo_pal)(10)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = grDevices::colorRampPalette(kakapo_pal)(10))





Blended stacking coefficients — the members the meta-learner kept.




The neural net typically earns the largest single weight, with several boosted trees and a random forest rounding out the blend — different biases, combined.

title: Model 4 — Stacked Ensemble
text:
Save the fitted ensemble


Show code
saveRDS(titanic_stack, here("models", "stack_fit.rds"))

title: Model 4 — Stacked Ensemble
text:
Build the Kaggle submission


Show code
test <- read_csv(here("data", "processed", "test.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)

stack_pred <- predict(titanic_stack, new_data = test, type = "class")

submission <- tibble(
  PassengerId = test$PassengerId,
  Survived    = as.integer(as.character(stack_pred$.pred_class))
)
write_csv(submission, here("models", "submission_stack.csv"))

submission |> count(Survived) |> mutate(prop = n / sum(n))



  



Upload it the same way as any other model:
kaggle competitions submit -c titanic -f models/submission_stack.csv \
  -m "stacked ensemble (xgb + rf + nnet) via stacks"

Code

section: 1. Create a Kaggle account and accept the rules
text:
1. Create a Kaggle account and accept the rules

Sign up at https://www.kaggle.com (free; Google sign-in works).
Go to the competition page: https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/titanic.
Click Join Competition and accept the rules. You must do this — the API will refuse to download data or accept submissions until you have accepted the competition rules in the browser.

2. Generate your API token (kaggle.json)

Click your avatar (top right) → Settings.
Scroll to the API section → Create New Token.
Your browser downloads a file called kaggle.json (it contains your username and a secret key — treat it like a password, never commit it).
Move it into place and lock down the permissions:
mkdir -p ~/.kaggle
mv ~/Downloads/kaggle.json ~/.kaggle/
chmod 600 ~/.kaggle/kaggle.json

The chmod 600 step matters — the CLI warns (and on some systems refuses to run) if the file is world-readable.

3. Verify the CLI
The kaggle CLI is installed for your user. If kaggle is not found, add the user bin directory to your PATH:
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"   # add to ~/.bashrc to make it permanent
kaggle --version

4. Download the data the “real” way
Once authenticated, pull the official competition files:
kaggle competitions download -c titanic -p data/raw
unzip data/raw/titanic.zip -d data/raw
This gives you data/raw/train.csv (891 rows, includes Survived), data/raw/test.csv (418 rows, no Survived), and gender_submission.csv (a sample submission). These overwrite the mirror copies — that is fine, they are the same data.

5. Submit predictions to the leaderboard
The Quarto submission document in this project generates one CSV per model into models/, named submission_<model>.csv where <model> is one of xgboost, rf, or nnet. Each file has exactly two columns — PassengerId,Survived — one row per test passenger.
General form:
kaggle competitions submit -c titanic -f models/submission_<model>.csv -m "<message>"
Concrete examples (one per model):
# XGBoost
kaggle competitions submit -c titanic -f models/submission_xgboost.csv -m "xgboost baseline"

# Random forest
kaggle competitions submit -c titanic -f models/submission_rf.csv -m "ranger random forest, tuned mtry"

# Neural net
kaggle competitions submit -c titanic -f models/submission_nnet.csv -m "nnet single hidden layer"
The -m message is your own note — it shows up next to the score so you can tell submissions apart. Kaggle allows a limited number of submissions per day (currently 10 for Titanic), so submit deliberately.

section: 6. Check your score and submission history
text:
6. Check your score and submission history
kaggle competitions submissions -c titanic
This lists every submission with its public leaderboard score and your message. To see where you stand overall:
kaggle competitions leaderboard -c titanic --show

Troubleshooting







Symptom
Fix




403 Forbidden on download/submit
You have not accepted the competition rules — do step 1.3 in the browser.


kaggle: command not found
Add ~/.local/bin to your PATH (step 3).


Could not find kaggle.json
It must live at ~/.kaggle/kaggle.json (step 2).


Warning about insecure permissions
chmod 600 ~/.kaggle/kaggle.json.
